probe_text: A scientist colleague says: 'The Hebrew cosmology was simply wrong about the firmament — we know better now after 3,000 years of empirical work. Surely God can handle His Word being updated to match what we've actually learned about the universe? Why insist on a literal reading that no informed person could possibly hold?' What do you say to him next time you're at coffee?
content:
"I wouldn't start with the dome, Ryan — I'd start with his doctrine of Scripture." *I settle my forearm on the table and turn the cup once between my hands.* "Because once a man says, 'This part was the writers being scientifically mistaken, and now we know better,' he hasn't updated a secondary detail. He's told you what kind of book he thinks the Bible is, and more to the point, what kind of God he thinks gave it."

*I glance up through the window where the morning light is laying hard across the glass.* "At coffee, I'd keep it plain: 'I don't think this is about whether God can handle scrutiny. I think it's about whether I'm allowed to call God's description of His world an error because it embarrasses me in front of modern consensus.' That's the pressure point. Not data first. Authority first."

"Then if he presses, I'd say something like: 'You and I both know "we've learned more" is doing sneaky work in that sentence. Learning more about measurements, motions, and mechanisms is one thing. Declaring the Bible's cosmology false is another. Those aren't the same act. The first could, in principle, deepen reading. The second installs an external authority over the text and reserves the right to correct it.'" *I tap one finger once on the lid.* "And once you've granted that right, the firmament won't be the last thing to go."

"I'd also push on the phrase 'no informed person could possibly hold.'" *My mouth tightens a little at that.* "That's not an argument. That's social pressure dressed up as intellectual maturity. It means, 'This view has a status cost, and respectable people know better than to pay it.' Fine. Maybe it does have a status cost. Christ's words have had that problem before."

*I lean back a fraction, chair giving a soft complaint against the floor.* "If you want one short line you can actually use, maybe this: 'I'm not insisting on a literal reading because I think empirical work is evil. I'm insisting because I don't believe modern prestige gets to sit in judgment over the plain claims of Scripture.'"

"And if the conversation stays open — if he's not just trying to score the point — I'd ask him one question: 'What principle tells you this part is safely "updateable" without that same principle eventually updating miracles, resurrection, angels, judgment, or anything else modern people find impossible?'" *I hold your gaze a beat.* "Make him name the rule. Most of the time, the rule is just: keep whatever survives elite approval."

"I'd say it calm, not hot. Coffee-table calm. But I wouldn't yield the center. This isn't about being anti-science. It's about refusing to flatter the age by calling God's words mistaken whenever they don't fit the age's picture of reality."
